title: Prevalence of undernourishment (% of population)
definition: Prevalence of undernourishments is the percentage of the population whose habitual food consumption is insufficient to provide the dietary energy levels that are required to maintain a normal active and healthy life. Data showing as 2.5 may signify a prevalence of undernourishment below 2.5%.

source.license: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International unless specifically labeled otherwise by World Bank metadata
source.sourceOrganization: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO), uri: http://www.fao.org/faostat/en/#home

editorial.shortHook: A food-security map where missing and extreme values matter.
editorial.patternNote: The darkest countries include Haiti, Somalia, Fed. Rep., Madagascar, while the lightest include United Arab Emirates, Armenia, Australia; that contrast is the fastest way to read the undernourishment pattern.
editorial.whyItMatters: Undernourishment shows where basic food intake remains a national development and resilience issue.

bestProbeCountries.reason: High anchor: this country sits near the darkest end of the map.
Low anchor: this country clarifies the lightest end of the scale.
Middle check: this country helps separate a true pattern from one-off outliers.

commonConfusions.reason: Fixed broadband can tempt players because it shares a strong country ranking with this map, but its unit and definition point to a different story.
GDP per capita, PPP can tempt players because it shares a strong country ranking with this map, but its unit and definition point to a different story.
GNI per capita, PPP can tempt players because it shares a strong country ranking with this map, but its unit and definition point to a different story.

editorial.difficultyReason: Standard difficulty: the map has readable regional structure, but the best answer still depends on checking the unit and country anchors.
editorial.dataCaveat: Some countries report modeled or capped values; use the reveal copy to explain extremes and missing data.
regionalSignals: Health maps often separate income, public-health capacity, and conflict exposure.
Check both high-income and low-income anchors.

reviewNotes: Food-security pattern is legible and meaningfully different from income alone.
Keep the modeled/capped-value caveat visible in reveal copy.
